sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 3 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT, LEFT, LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 75, level 2.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 44 of about 123 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Something unexpected happened! The target is now at rows 40-41, cols 29-33, not at rows 10-11, cols 14-18 as I expected. It seems like the LEFT actions caused the target to wrap around or teleport to a different position.
Wait, looking at the ascii output:
- Rows 10-14: GGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGG (all G)
- Rows 15-20: GGGGGGGGGGGGGGcccccG
The target is at rows 40-41, cols 29-33. This is the same row range as before, but the columns changed from 34-38 to 29-33. So the LEFT actions moved the target from cols 34-38 to cols 29-33, but the target is now at rows 40-41 instead of rows 10-11.
Wait, that doesn't make sense. The target was at rows 10-11, cols 29-33 before the LEFT actions. After LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, the target should be at rows 10-11, cols 14-18. But the target is at rows 40-41, cols 29-33.
Oh wait, I see what happened. The target wrapped around or teleported. Or maybe the LEFT actions from rows 10-11, cols 29-33 moved the target to a different position.
Actually, looking more carefully at the ascii:
- Row 10: GGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGG (20 G's, cols 10-29)
- Row 11: GGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGG (20 G's, cols 10-29)
The target is not visible in rows 10-11. This means the target moved out of that area. The target is now at rows 40-41, cols 29-33.
Hmm, this is strange. Let me think about what happened.
- Before LEFT actions: target at rows 10-11, cols 29-33
- After LEFT, LEFT, LEFT: target at rows 40-41, cols 29-33
Wait, maybe the LEFT actions from rows 10-11, cols 29-33 moved the target to cols 24-28, then cols 19-23, then cols 14-18. But the ascii shows the target at rows 40-41, cols 29-33.
Actually, I think there might be a wrap-around or teleport mechanic. When the target moves LEFT from cols 14-18, it might wrap around to the right side of the maze. Or maybe the target teleports to a different position when it reaches a certain boundary.
Let me look at the segmentation output more carefully:
- Target: [[40, 29], [40, 33], [41, 33], [41, 29]]
- Yellow: [[16, 15], [16, 17], [18, 17], [18, 15]] (yellow marker)
- Yellow: [[51, 40], [51, 42], [53, 42], [53, 40]] (another yellow marker)
- Yellow: [[61, 13], [61, 54], [62, 54], [62, 13]] (this is the HUD bar at the bottom)
So there are two yellow markers: one at rows 16-18, cols 15-17 and one at rows 51-53, cols 40-42.
The target is at rows 40-41, cols 29-33. This is close to the second yellow marker at rows 51-53, cols 40-42. Maybe the goal is to move the target to the second yellow marker?
Let me try moving DOWN and RIGHT to reach the second yellow marker.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Target at rows 40-41, cols 29-33
# Yellow marker at rows 51-53, cols 40-42
# Let me try to reach it by moving DOWN and RIGHT
# DOWN from rows 40-41 moves to rows 45-46
# RIGHT from cols 29-33 moves to cols 34-38
# Let me try DOWN, RIGHT
result = action(['DOWN', 'RIGHT'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'O':
        print(f"Target: {n['boundary']}, level_completed={result.get('level_completed')}")

# Check yellow markers
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'Y':
        print(f"Yellow: {n['boundary']}")

# Print maze area around target
ascii = current_frame.ascii
lines = ascii.split('\n')
print("\nMaze area (rows 35-55, cols 25-50):")
for i, line in enumerate(lines):
    if 35 <= i <= 55 and i < len(lines):
        print(f"{i:2d}: {line[25:50]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
